findings.title: Unauthenticated proxy endpoint with no input length limits
findings.description: GET /api/audible/proxy requires no authentication and applies no length constraints on title, author, num_results, or response_groups query params. An attacker can send arbitrarily long strings for any param (especially response_groups), causing URLSearchParams to construct a very large URL forwarded to api.audible.com. While the target URL is hardcoded, this can be used to waste server resources or probe Audible API rate-limit behaviour. num_results is passed through without validation — a value like '1000' will be forwarded verbatim.
findings.remediation: Add input validation: max length 200 chars for title/author, whitelist numeric check for num_results (clamp 1-25), whitelist allowed response_groups values. Consider requiring JWT auth (BYOK tier) even for public APIs to prevent abuse from non-users.

findings.title: Server-side request to unauthenticated external API leaks server IP and can be rate-abused
findings.description: The proxy unconditionally forwards requests to api.audible.com using the server's IP address. No caching layer exists, so each client search triggers a new outbound request. Repeated calls by unauthenticated clients will exhaust any Audible rate limit allocated to the server IP and could result in the server IP being blocked by Audible. Additionally, the endpoint has no per-IP rate limiter unlike other /api/ai/* routes that use createRateLimiter.
findings.remediation: Apply a rate limiter (e.g. 10 req/min per client IP) to this endpoint, consistent with other proxy routes. Add a short-lived server-side cache keyed on title+author.

findings.title: Unvalidated external cover URL fetched and converted client-side
findings.description: result.coverUrl is sourced from external API responses (Google Books, iTunes, Audnexus, Open Library) and passed directly to fetch() without URL validation. While browser CSP restricts connect-src, a malicious or compromised API could return a data: URL (not restricted by img-src connect-src in all browsers), a javascript: URL, or a URL redirecting to an internal network resource. The fetched blob is drawn onto a canvas, then stored — if the browser's image decoder has a vulnerability this is a vector. The CSP does not include a wildcard SSRF concern but data: URIs bypass domain checks.
findings.remediation: Validate coverUrl before fetching: reject non-https:// schemes, check that hostname matches the known whitelist of cover CDN domains (covers.openlibrary.org, *.mzstatic.com, m.media-amazon.com, books.google.com). Reject data: and javascript: schemes explicitly.

findings.title: CSP connect-src includes api.audible.com and m.media-amazon.com — broad Amazon surface
findings.description: Adding m.media-amazon.com to both img-src and connect-src opens a broad Amazon domain. m.media-amazon.com hosts many asset types and subdomains. If a future code path inadvertently fetches non-cover resources from this origin (account data, tracking pixels), the CSP would not block it. Similarly, api.audible.com in connect-src allows browser-direct XHR to the full Audible API surface, not just catalog/products.
findings.remediation: Narrow the CSP to the specific path patterns needed. For img-src m.media-amazon.com is appropriate. For connect-src, remove api.audible.com if all Audible calls route through the server-side proxy; browser-direct calls to api.audible.com are the fallback path but the proxy exists precisely to avoid them.
